coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the sensitive determination of larotrectinib in biological fluids and dosage forms: Evaluation of method greenness and selectivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Alossaimi, Manal A.
Altamimi, Abdulmalik S.A.
Elmansi, Heba
Magdy, Galal
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the kinase receptor inhibitor drug larotrectinib has been approved as a monotherapy for the treatment of patients with solid tumors containing the neurotrophic receptor tyrosine kinase gene fusion. In this paper, a novel sensitive spectrofluorimetric method was proposed for the determination of larotrectinib based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) fluorescent probes. The proposed method is the first spectroscopic method for analysis of the cited drug, which is simple to implement and involves no pre-treatment steps or complicated techniques. The N-CQDs synthesis was performed by adopting a straightforward, fast, and environmentally friendly approach. It was achieved by means of a standard domestic microwave with inexpensive and readily available starting materials: orange juice (carbon source) and urea (nitrogen source). The synthesized N-CQDs were subjected to microscopic and spectroscopic characterization procedures. They were found to be stable with a sufficiently high fluorescence quantum yield (25.3%) and a small particle size distribution (2–5 nm). The motivation for the use of N-CQDs in this study arose from their excellent fluorescence intensities at 417 nm when excited at 325 nm. Larotrectinib was found to have a quantitative and selective quenching effect on the QDs fluorescence allowing for its sensitive determination. The drug’s quenching mechanism was investigated and found to be of the static type. Under optimal conditions, the proposed approach permitted the determination of larotrectinib over the concentration interval of 5.0–28.0 µg/mL. The method showed sufficient sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.19 µg/mL and a quantitation limit of 0.57 µg/mL, enabling the determination of LARO in spiked human plasma samples. The approach’s recovery percentage was found to be in the range of 99.09–100.73% for pure samples and 97.35–102.59% for plasma samples. The study also successfully applied the proposed approach to the commercial oral solution form of larotrectinib (Vitrakvi®) with high selectivity. Method greenness was further evaluated by adopting two metric tools, including the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) and Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE), and it was confirmed to be excellent green. The proposed method was validated in accordance with the ICHQ2 (R1) recommendations and is considered an excellent candidate for potential application in the therapeutic monitoring of larotrectinib.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Larotrectinib
Quantum dots
Nanosensors
Quenching
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of LARO in prepared oral solution
Analysis of LARO in spiked human plasma
Characterization of N-CQDs and the quenching mechanism
Validation of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method
Application of the proposed method to LARO oral solutions
Analysis of LARO in spiked human plasma samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported via funding from Prince Sattam Bin Abdulaziz University , project number PSAU/2023/R/1444.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A). Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs; (a) excitation and (b) emission, (B). Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (310–380 nm).

(A). Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs upon addition of different concentrations of LARO (from top to bottom: 0, 5.0, 8.0, 12.0, 16.0, 20.0, 24.0, 28.0 μg/mL), (B). The linearity plot of fluorescence quenching against LARO concentration.
Stern-Volmer plots for the quenching of N-CQDs fluorescence by LARO at four distinct temperatures (298, 303, 313, and 323 K).
Results of greenness assessment of the proposed method using ComplexGAPI (A) and AGREE (B) tools in pharmaceutical preparation (1) and plasma samples (2).
Synthesis of N-CQDs and their application for the determination of LARO.
Validation data for LARO by the proposed spectrofluorimetric method.
Application of the designed method for the determination of LARO in pure form.
Application of the spectrofluorimetric method for LARO determination in spiked human plasma samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the sensitive determination of larotrectinib in biological fluids and dosage forms: Evaluation of method greenness and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Department, College of Pharmacy, Prince Sattam bin Abdulaziz University, Al-Kharj 11942, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Department, College of Pharmacy, Prince Sattam bin Abdulaziz University, Al-Kharj 11942, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura P.O. Box 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura P.O. Box 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh P.O. Box 33511, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh P.O. Box 33511, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the kinase receptor inhibitor drug larotrectinib has been approved as a monotherapy for the treatment of patients with solid tumors containing the neurotrophic receptor tyrosine kinase gene fusion. In this paper, a novel sensitive spectrofluorimetric method was proposed for the determination of larotrectinib based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) fluorescent probes. The proposed method is the first spectroscopic method for analysis of the cited drug, which is simple to implement and involves no pre-treatment steps or complicated techniques. The N-CQDs synthesis was performed by adopting a straightforward, fast, and environmentally friendly approach. It was achieved by means of a standard domestic microwave with inexpensive and readily available starting materials: orange juice (carbon source) and urea (nitrogen source). The synthesized N-CQDs were subjected to microscopic and spectroscopic characterization procedures. They were found to be stable with a sufficiently high fluorescence quantum yield (25.3%) and a small particle size distribution (2–5 nm). The motivation for the use of N-CQDs in this study arose from their excellent fluorescence intensities at 417 nm when excited at 325 nm. Larotrectinib was found to have a quantitative and selective quenching effect on the QDs fluorescence allowing for its sensitive determination. The drug’s quenching mechanism was investigated and found to be of the static type. Under optimal conditions, the proposed approach permitted the determination of larotrectinib over the concentration interval of 5.0–28.0 µg/mL. The method showed sufficient sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.19 µg/mL and a quantitation limit of 0.57 µg/mL, enabling the determination of LARO in spiked human plasma samples. The approach’s recovery percentage was found to be in the range of 99.09–100.73% for pure samples and 97.35–102.59% for plasma samples. The study also successfully applied the proposed approach to the commercial oral solution form of larotrectinib (Vitrakvi®) with high selectivity. Method greenness was further evaluated by adopting two metric tools, including the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) and Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE), and it was confirmed to be excellent green. The proposed method was validated in accordance with the ICHQ2 (R1) recommendations and is considered an excellent candidate for potential application in the therapeutic monitoring of larotrectinib.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Larotrectinib (LARO) is a kinase receptor inhibitor with a chemical molecular formula of C21H22F2N6O2 and a molecular weight of 428.4 g/mol. Its chemical name, according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) is (3S)-N-[5-[(2R)-2-(2,5-difluorophenyl)pyrrolidin-1-yl]pyrazolo[1,5-a]pyrimidin-3-yl]-3-hydroxypyrrolidine-1-carboxamide (Fig. 1 ) [1]. Its commercial dosage form manufactured by Bayer AG has the trade name Vitrakvi®. This commercial drug is available in an oral capsule form (100.0 mg, 25.0 mg) as well as an oral solution (20.0 mg/mL). LARO was approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in November 2018 as a monotherapy for the treatment of pediatric and adult patients with solid tumors containing the neurotrophic receptor tyrosine kinase gene fusion [2].

Over the last few years, a few analytical methods have been proposed in the literature for the determination and analysis of LARO. A high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) method was proposed to quantify LARO in mouse blood [3]. A similar LC-MS/MS method was developed for the estimation of LARO, along with an evaluation of its metabolic stability [4]. HPLC-MS/MS was also used to study the stability of LARO sulfate under stress conditions [5]. Due to the complexity and high cost of these methods, a simpler HPLC method was proposed and used to quantify LARO in mice plasma. This method also presented a pharmacokinetic study in mice and showed a reasonable sensitivity [6].

The abovementioned chromatographic approaches require the use of complicated equipment and large volumes of harmful organic solvents as well as well-trained personnel. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, no simpler or green methods, such as those based on spectrofluorimetric analysis, have been proposed and validated for the quantitation of the cited drug. Hence, we were motivated to investigate a rapid, cost-effective, uncomplicated, and ecofriendly spectrofluorimetric determination method for LARO, which does not require sophisticated equipment or pretreatment. The novelty of this study is addressed in being the first spectroscopic approach for the analysis of the cited drug. Spectrofluorimetry is a simple and attractive analytical tool capable of achieving comparable selectivity and sensitivity to HPLC and LC-MS, allowing for the sensitive estimation of many pharmaceutical compounds in different matrices [7–13]. The current study focused on the development and validation of a novel fluorescence quenching-based approach for the estimation of LARO in spiked human plasma. The developed method was also used to quantify the commercial Vitrakvi® oral solution form of the drug and was shown to be practical and highly selective. Therefore, the proposed method is deemed to be suitable for direct application in the therapeutic monitoring of LARO and its routine analysis in quality control laboratories.

To fulfill the objectives of this study, quantum dots (QDs) were used as fluorescent nanosensors, which formed the basis of the proposed spectrofluorimetric procedure. QDs are nano-scale fluorescent particles that were originally made from semiconductor materials. They possess several interesting optical and electrical characteristics, including wide excitation spectra and narrow, tunable, and symmetric emission spectra. In addition, they have been shown to exhibit excellent stability against photobleaching [14]. For these reasons, they have been widely adopted as a replacement for conventional organic dyes. They have been used in numerous applications within the pharmaceutical field. One interesting application of QDs is the detection, analysis, and quantitation of drugs by taking advantage of the quenching effect they have on the QDs' native fluorescence emission [15].

The semiconductor material used to construct QDs was mainly abandoned for carbon, as it has a better environmental impact, lower toxicity and cost, in addition to more facile synthesis. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with high aqueous solubility, stable photoluminescence, and strong biocompatibility, have been reported for analysis of several drugs and in different matrices [16]. Furthermore, other advantages, including enhancing the optical and electrical properties, were further added to these nanoparticles by heteroatom doping using nitrogen, fluorine, boron, or sulfur [15,17–20]. Due to the aforementioned features of QDs and the fact that they are water-soluble, they are being used as fluorescent probes in the quantitation of various drugs, including dacomitinib [21], palbociclib [22], lidocaine [23], salinomycin and maduramicin [24], olanzapine and diazepam [25], glibenclamide [26], saxagliptin and gliclazide [27], and gemcitabine [28]. In this study, nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were synthesized from orange juice as a green C- source and urea as an N- source through a green and simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis technique, and they were utilized as fluorescent probes in the proposed spectrofluorimetric procedure for LARO determination. Furthermore, the proposed approach's greenness profile was evaluated using two metric tools, including the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [29] and the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE) [30], and it was confirmed to be an excellent green approach.

Among the instruments used to conduct the experiments required by the current study are the following:
-
The Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, United States) with a Xenon flash lamp was used at 750 V.
-
Double beam spectrophotometer acquired from PG Instruments, UK.
-
The Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer from ThermoFisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA) along with a 4000 to 1000 cm−1 DTGS detector and a Ge/KBr beam splitter. Each measurement was taken in 32 scans with a resolution of 4 cm−1.
-
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were carried out using the JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) by JEOL (Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 Kv.
-
The Sigma 2-16P benchtop refrigerated centrifuge (Germany).
-
The VM-300P vortex mixer from Gemmy Industrial Corp (Taiwan).
-
Membrane filters (0.45 µm, Phenomenex, USA).
-
Domestic Microwave (GE614ST, 2800 W, 2450 MHz, Samsung, Malaysia).

All materials and reagents used in the experimental part of this study were of analytical grade. Also, double distilled water was used throughout, and all chemicals were used directly without the need for any further purification. The following is a list of the most important materials and solutions used in the experiments:
-
LARO (99.65%) was procured in pure powder form from MedChemExpress (Monmouth Junction, NJ 08852, USA).
-
Methanol was obtained from Tedia (Fairfield, United States).
-
Urea was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).
-
Navel orange (Species Citrus sinensis) was attained from the local Egyptian market.

LARO stock solution was prepared in methanol with an initial concentration of 100.0 µg/mL. Subsequently, appropriate dilutions of the solution were made with double distilled water to obtain the required concentrations (5.0–28.0 µg/mL). In addition, 0.2 M of the Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared in distilled water with pH values in the range of 2–12.

As mentioned earlier, we have elected to use N-CQDs as the basis of the proposed spectrofluorimetric determination method. This type of QDs is simple to synthesize and is generally an environmentally friendly fluorescent probe compared to the standard organic dyes. A mixture of orange juice (50.0 mL) and urea (3.0 gm) was placed into a standard domestic microwave for a duration of 10 min. The resulting burnt substance was cooled and diluted to 100.0 mL with distilled water before being centrifuged for a duration of 15 min at the rotation rate of 6000 rpm. A clear layer that formed above the residue was separated by means of a filter and completed with distilled water to 200.0 mL. This yielded the N-CQDs stock solution. The working solution was then obtained by diluting a 10.0 mL portion of the stock solution with double distilled water to fill a 100.0 mL volumetric flask. The solutions were stored in a refrigerator for further use. The whole procedure is depicted in Scheme 1 .

After parameter optimization, small portions of the N-CQDs solution with a volume of 125.0 µL were placed into a set of 5.0 mL volumetric flasks. Appropriate varying volumes of the LARO working solution were added to the flasks and completed with distilled water to produce concentrations in the studied range of 5.0–28.0 µg/mL. The resulting solutions were excited at a wavelength of 325 nm, and the fluorescence emissions were observed at 417 nm, according to the intrinsic properties of the N-CQDs. The same excitation and emission wavelengths were adopted for the blank experiments. The quenching effect of LARO on the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs was calculated and graphed against the drug concentration in µg/mL. This yielded the calibration curve for the proposed analysis method.

The quantum yield (QY) of a fluorescent probe, denoted by
Φ
, is a measure of its photoemissive efficiency. In this study, quinine sulfate (QS) dissolved in 0.1 M of H2SO4 was used as the standard for QY calculations. It has a quantum yield of
Φ
Q S
= 0.54
at 350 nm. The QY of the synthesized N-CQDs was calculated according to the following Eq. (1) [31]:
(1)
Φ
N - C Q D s
=
Φ
Q S
×
F
N - C Q D s
/
F
Q S
×
η
N - C Q D s
/
η
Q S
2
×
A
Q S
/
A
N - C Q D s
,
Where, the parameters
F
,
η
, and
A
denote the integrated emission intensity, the solvent refractive index, and the absorbance, respectively. For the case at hand, the ratio
η
N - C Q D s
/
η
Q S
= 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of LARO in prepared oral solution
ce:section.ce:para: 10.0 mL of the LARO oral solution, with a concentration of 20.0 mg/mL, was laboratory prepared by mixing with the excipients: sucrose, sorbitol, propylene glycol, and parahydroxybenzoate. The mixture was then completed to 10.0 mL with distilled water [32,33]. The resulting solution was placed into a 100.0 mL volumetric flask along with 80 mL of methanol and sonicated for 10 min in an ultrasonic bath. The flask was then completed with methanol, and its contents were then filtered. Different aliquots of the solution were then transferred into a series of 5.0 mL volumetric flasks to produce concentrations within the required range. Subsequently, the procedures under Section ''2.5′' were followed. Finally, the contents of the oral solution were determined using the regression equation of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of LARO in spiked human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Portions of human plasma with a volume of 1.0 mL were placed into a series of centrifugation tubes (15.0 mL). Appropriate Aliquots of the LARO stock solution were added to the tubes to yield linearly distributed concentrations in the range of 5.0–8.0 µg/mL. After being mixed in the vortexer for a duration of 2 min, the tubes were completed to 5.0 mL with methanol as the plasma protein precipitating agent. The tubes were then placed into the cooling centrifuge for 15 min with the rotational speed set at 6000 rpm. Subsequently, 1.0 mL portions of the supernatant were taken from the tubes and placed into a sequence of 5.0 mL volumetric flasks after being passed through 0.45 µm syringe filters. 125.0 µL aliquots of the synthesized N-CQDs solution were added to each flask and completed with distilled water to the mark. The LARO concentration in each flask was measured by means of the proposed procedure as described in Section ''2.5′' with a blank experiment in parallel. The fluorescence quenching level corresponding to each concentration was recorded and plotted against the drug concentrations. The linear regression was used to produce a representative formula from which percentage recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CQDs and the quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
When the synthesized N-CQDs solution was subjected to UV light, it exhibited a stable, bright blue fluorescence, as shown in Figure S1. A comprehensive scan of the UV spectrum was carried out, and N-CQDs were found to have two characteristic absorption bands at the wavelengths
λ
m a x
= 213 n m
and
λ
m a x
= 275 n m
, as shown in Figure S2, which may be attributed, respectively, to the
π
-
π
∗
and
n
-
π
∗
transitions [34]. In addition, Fig. 2
A shows that the N-CQDs exhibit strong emission fluorescence intensity at 417 nm after excitation at 325 nm with a high quantum yield of 25.3%. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the emission of synthesized QDs showed excitation dependence in the 310 to 380 nm range, with the best fluorescence emission occurring at 325 nm. Additionally, the prepared N-CQDs displayed a lengthy homogenous phase, no discernible precipitation, and stable fluorescence intensity for more than four months.

To assess the physical shape and structure of the N-CQDs, pictures were taken with the aid of HRTEM. From the image shown in Figure S3, spherical shapes with a diameter in the range of 2–5 nm were observed. The quantum dots seem to be separated, and no aggregation was observed. The FTIR spectrum corresponding to the synthesized N-CQDs is depicted in Figure S4. Five peaks are observed corresponding to OH/NH (3500–3100 cm−1), CN (2097 cm−1), CO (1701 cm−1), CC (1658 cm−1), and CH (595 cm−1). The prepared N-CQDs were also subjected to an elemental analysis using EDX in order to determine their composition and confirm the degree of nitrogen doping. According to the findings, the N-CQDs were primarily composed of carbon (59.21%), oxygen (24.70%), and nitrogen (16.08%), demonstrating that a high nitrogen doping level was attained (Figure S5).

As shown in Fig. 3 , the native fluorescence of N-CQDs was quantitatively quenched as LARO concentration was increased. One way to identify the mechanism by which LARO quenches the intrinsic fluorescence of the N-CQDs is to use the Stern-Volmer formula [35,36]. The formula states that:
(2)
F
0
/
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
Q

In this context,
F
0
and
F
denote the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs solution in the absence and presence of LARO, respectively. In addition,
K
sv
is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant,
Q
is the LARO concentration,
K
q
denotes the quenching rate constant, and
τ
0
is the average lifetime of the fluorophore (10−8 s). The fluorescence intensity quenching ratio
F
0
/
F
was graphed as a function of the molar concentration of LARO, as presented in Fig. 4 . The formula was applied to data taken at four different temperatures 298, 303, 313, and 323 K. The corresponding values of
K
sv
were found to be 1.15 × 104, 1.08 × 104, 9.96 × 103, and 9.20 × 103 L.mol −1, respectively. The decrease in
K
sv
as temperature rises suggests a static quenching process. The corresponding values of
K
q
were calculated to be 1.15 × 1012, 1.08 × 1012, 9.96 × 1011, and 9.20 × 1011 L.mol−1.s−1, respectively. The fact that
K
q
is much larger than the maximum diffusion rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L.mol−1.s−1) confirms the suggested static quenching process [17].
Two main parameters that may potentially affect the efficiency of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method were investigated as described below.

A Britton–Robinson buffer (0.02 M) was used to vary the pH level of the working solutions within the range of 2–12. Measurements taken over the stated pH range showed no significant change in the fluorescence quenching; thus the pH was not an influential parameter, and no buffer was required for the study.

Experiments were carried out whereby the proposed procedure’s incubation time was varied over the interval of 1–60 min. The reaction between LARO and the N-CQDs was found to conclude in less than 1 min, and the fluorescence quenching measurements remained stable for more than 60 min, which added another advantage to the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: The International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) has issued a set of guidelines that ought to be considered when developing a new procedure for laboratory implementation. The guidelines include an investigation of the method’s linearity, range, limit of quantitation (LOQ), limit of detection (LOD), precision, accuracy, and selectivity [37]. In the current study, we adhered to the guidelines, as will be shown in this section.

The fluorescence quenching caused by LARO was measured for different drug concentrations in the range of 5.0–28.0 µg/mL and used to construct the calibration curve for the proposed method. The response of the method over the stated range was found to be highly linear, with the regression equation being
y
= 9.74
x
- 8.26
, and the correlation coefficient being r = 0.9999 (Fig. 3).

The sensitivity of the method was examined through the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ), which are given, respectively, by
LOD = 3.3
S
a
/
b
and
LOQ = 10
S
a
/
b
, where
S
a
is the standard deviation of the intercept and
b
is the slope of the regression curve. The two measures were calculated to be
LOD = 0.19 μ g / m L
and
LOQ = 0.57 μ g / m L
, which is indicative of the proposed method being highly sensitive. Hence, the method is deemed to be suitable for the determination of LARO in human plasma. Table 1 summarizes the analytical performance data for the developed methodology.

The accuracy of the proposed method was quantified by means of the recovery percentage and relative error. Seven different concentrations of the pure form of LARO spanning the interval of interest (5.0–28.0 µg/mL) were considered. The concentrations measured by the proposed method in absolute and percentage forms are listed in Table 2 . The accuracy of the proposed approach was satisfactory, as revealed by the high % recoveries (99.09–100.73) and low % error (<0.3).

The precision of the proposed method was assessed by considering the determination of three distinct LARO concentrations (8.0, 16.0, and 24.0 µg/mL) and repeating the process thrice in one day (intra-day) and over three consecutive days (inter-day). The mean, standard deviation, relative standard deviation, and relative error of the inter- and intra-day measurements are listed in Table S1. The results confirmed that the method has an acceptable precision, as evidenced by the low % RSD values (≤1.051 %).

Finally, the selectivity of the proposed method was assessed by examining its ability to estimate LARO in its oral solution (Vitrakvi®) with high % recovery (98.73–101.39%) and low %RSD (≤1.273%) values without any interference from existing excipients (Table S2). Mannitol, dextrin, lactose, maltose, and citric acid were investigated in-depth to rule out the possibility of interference and confirmed the method's high selectivity because they almost had no effect on the N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6A). The selectivity of the method was similarly demonstrated by its unaffected detection of the studied drug in the presence of various metal ions, such as Ca+2, Mg+2, Ba+2, Na+, and K+ (Fig. S6B). The developed approach was also able to determine LARO in the presence of other anticancer drugs, such as dacomitinib and palbociclib. The tolerance limit for these medications was established as the concentration that causes a 2% or greater relative error [27], which was found to be 1.0 µg/mL for both medications. In addition, the proposed method’s selectivity was confirmed by determining LARO in complicated plasma matrices. The method achieved reasonably high recovery percentages in the range of 97.35–102.59% without any considerable interference from the plasma components (Table 3 ). The developed method consequently demonstrated excellent selectivity for LARO determination without any interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed method to LARO oral solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed spectrofluorimetric method was efficiently applied to the Vitrakvi® oral solution described earlier in Section ''2.6.''. The added excipients did not have a noticeable effect on the readings, and the method remained satisfactorily selective, accurate, and precise. Table S2 listed the results when five different LARO concentrations were analyzed by the suggested method. The obtained high recovery percentage (98.73–101.39%) and low relative standard deviation (≤1.273%) confirm the high selectivity of the approach. Therefore, the proposed method could be applied for the routine analysis of LARO in its oral solution and can be easily adopted in quality control laboratories.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of LARO in spiked human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para:
As the previous experiments showed good selectivity levels for the proposed spectrofluorimetric method, we were motivated to consider a more vigorous scenario whereby the method was used to analyze LARO concentrations in spiked human plasma samples. The peak plasma concentration
C
max
measured 30 min after the oral administration of LARO at the dosage of 20.0 mg/kg was reported in the literature to be 8.14 µg/mL [3]. Due to the high sensitivity of the proposed approach (down to 0.19 µg/mL), it could be applied for the sensitive estimation of LARO in plasma samples. In our experiment, four different concentrations of LARO were considered, as shown in Table 3. The obtained absolute and relative concentrations are listed in the table, along with the corresponding statistical measures. Linear regression yields the regression equation:
y
= 23.9
x
- 76.6
with an acceptable correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9887. The obtained recovery (97.35–102.59%) and error (≤1.168%) percentages confirmed the validity of the method. Therefore, the developed method may hold potential applications in the therapeutic monitoring of LARO.

Globally, there is growing recognition of the significance of managing waste and hazards to enhance green analysis. In this study, two green metric tools were used to estimate the greenness profile of the proposed method, including the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [29] and the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE) [30]. ComplexGAPI is a novel greenness assessment tool that relies on GAPI [38] by adding an additional hexagonal field at its bottom. ComplexGAPI is required to cover the whole range of parameters that characterize the analytical protocol. It outlines the procedures followed before sample preparation and final analysis, including the yield and environmental factors, the connection to the green economy, equipment, workup, chemicals and solvents, and purification. The GAPI pictogram of ComplexGAPI has a hexagon with each field standing for a different step in the specified process. The associated criteria have been satisfied if the hexagon is green. The ComplexGAPI was successfully used to estimate the greenness of the proposed method (Fig. 5
A). The suggested strategy provides the advantages of a straightforward sample pre-treatment (protein precipitation). It is a micro-scale extraction resulting in great sensitivity. Due to the use of mild conditions and the minimal amount of chemical and reagent waste produced, the procedure appears to be environmentally friendly. Orange juice was used as a green carbon source in the N-CQDs' synthetic process. In addition, the spectrofluorimeter consumes little energy. Concerning AGREE, it is a tool for identifying the environmental and occupational hazards included in the analytical procedure by evaluating 12 important criteria. The final outcome ranges from 0 to 1.0. Fig. 5 B illustrates how the approach resulted in a high score and satisfactory results, demonstrating excellent green methodology.

LARO is the first selective small-molecule pan-TRK inhibitor approved by the US FDA for the treatment of solid cancerous tumors. Upon surveying the literature related to the drug, no simple and environmentally friendly quantitation methods were found, which motivated us to propose the first spectrofluorimetric approach for its estimation based on the use of N-CQDs as a fluorescent probe. The main idea is that the intrinsic fluorescence of the N-CQDs is quenched specifically and quantitatively by the increasing concentrations of LARO. N-CQDs were synthesized in our laboratory by means of an extremely simple, fast, and green approach that used a standard domestic microwave and the rather abundant starting materials: orange juice and urea. The synthesized QDs were characterized prior to their use in the proposed method and were found to possess satisfactory properties. In addition to analyzing LARO in its pure and oral solution forms, the proposed method was extended to quantifying LARO in human plasma samples and achieved good results. Hence, the proposed method is a novel, cost-effective, and sensitive fluorescent assay that uses simple procedures and does not require any prior treatment or complicated steps. For these reasons, the approach has the potential for application in therapeutic LARO drug monitoring.
This study was supported via funding from Prince Sattam Bin Abdulaziz University, project number PSAU/2023/R/1444.

Manal A. Alossaimi: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Abdulmalik S.A. Altamimi: Conceptualization, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Heba Elmansi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Galal Magdy: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122914.

ce:section.ce:para:
